title: Native California
subtitle: Approximate homelands of the nations of Alta California at contact
abstract: The nations whose country the other maps on this site cross. Each labeled circle marks an approximate homeland center, deliberately drawn without boundary lines: territories interlocked and shifted, the sources disagree, and hard lines on a map would claim a precision nobody has. Groupings follow the language families of Kroeber's Handbook (1925) as a conventional key; linguists now treat the larger groupings (Hokan, Penutian) as unproven, and Chumashan and Esselen as families of their own. These are living nations. Names use current preferred forms with mission-era names in parentheses. See also the Native Peoples Index for the named individuals and communities in this site's sources.
legend_note: Circles are approximate homeland centers, not boundaries.

features.name: Yurok
features.summary: Lower Klamath River and the adjacent coast.

sources.citation: After A. L. Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California (1925), and standard references; homeland centers approximate.

features.notes: The circle marks an approximate homeland center, not a boundary. Territories interlocked, shifted, and are still contested; for living communities' own maps, see each nation's tribal government.

features.name: Chumash
features.summary: The Santa Barbara Channel, its islands, and the coast from Malibu to San Luis Obispo. Maritime towns, plank canoes, and the largest mission-era revolt (1824). Kroeber grouped Chumashan with Hokan; it is now treated as its own family.

features.name: Kumeyaay (Diegueño)
features.summary: The San Diego country and northern Baja California, on both sides of the later border. From the 1775 destruction of Mission San Diego to the Jacum battle of 1837, the most persistent military resistance on the southern frontier. Yuman family.

features.name: Quechan (Yuma)
features.summary: The Colorado River crossing at the Gila junction. Their 1781 victory destroyed two Spanish settlements and closed the overland road to California for the rest of the colonial period. Yuman family.

features.name: Mojave
